Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed nominal metaphor; adjective/participle-like modifier + noun, used predicatively or as a vocative insult.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A foolish, scatterbrained, or reckless person who acts without thinking.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: an unintelligent or careless person; someone who does not think first
Usage Contexts: daily speech, reprimands, humor, literature, workplace mistakes
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Animal-based rural metaphor: the 'headless gadfly/horsefly' image fits traditional village speech where insects and livestock were familiar evaluative symbols.
First Example Sentence: Тудо планым лудде пӓшӓшкы пурен, старшийшӹ «вуйдымы пармы» манын кӹрдыжӹм каштӹш.
Second Example Sentence: Ешӹштӹ вуйдымы пармы гань кычкырен кошташ ак келеш.
Third Example Sentence: Вуйдымы пармы лияш ок шуй: йодышым ончыч умылаш келеш.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed simile; noun + comparative particle гань + finite/weather verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: It is raining extremely hard; a downpour is happening.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: a very heavy rain is falling
Usage Contexts: weather talk, travel, village life, daily conversation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in Volga-side rural imagery; ваштыр refers to twigs/rods or a broom-like object, making the rain feel dense and lashing.
First Example Sentence: Касеш ваштыр гань юреш, седӹндоно мӓ школ гыч вашке мӧнгӹ пӧртылна.
Second Example Sentence: Ваштыр гань юреш гӹнят, почта кандашлык эдем корным ок шӹмӹ.
Third Example Sentence: Иргодым ваштыр гань юреш, пӱнчер вӹлнӹ вӹд йогын шинча.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adverbial participial phrase; object noun + converb, modifying an action verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To work energetically, seriously, and without idleness.
Usage Contexts: workplace, farming, study, project management, motivational speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Manual-labor metaphor from rolling up one's sleeves before fieldwork, construction, household repair, or communal work.
First Example Sentence: Проектын пытартыш недельыштӹ мӓ шокш кӓргӓлтен ӹштенӓ.
Second Example Sentence: Кугу ӹшкӹмӹш деч посна, тудо садеш шокш кӓргӓлтен пӓшӓм тӹнгалын.
Third Example Sentence: Документвлӓм шокш кӓргӓлтен тӹрлӹ анжыктыда гӹнь, хата шагал лиеш.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound object + infinitive verb; body-part coordination with a causative/downward motion verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To lose heart, stop trying, or give up because of failure or hardship.
Usage Contexts: encouragement, family advice, workplace setbacks, school, sports
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Bodily metaphor: lowered hands and feet symbolize loss of agency, a common pattern in Mari and neighboring languages.
First Example Sentence: Ик гана янгылыш лиеш гӹньат, кид-ялым валташ ак кел.
Second Example Sentence: Команда отчетым пӹтӓрен огыл, но кид-ялым валтен шӹнзӓш вес йонгылыш лиеш.
Third Example Sentence: Авай эргӹжӹлан: «Тый кид-ялым ит валте, эче гана тӹнгал», мане.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive verb phrase; accusative object + infinitive verb, often inflected for person and tense.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To regain confidence, stand up for oneself, or begin acting independently.
Usage Contexts: social change, personal growth, education, politics, recovery after hardship
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The raised-head image evokes dignity after poverty, silence, or dependence; it is also useful in public or collective narratives.
First Example Sentence: У конкурсеш кудалтенӓ гӹнь, йочавлӓ вуйым лӱктӓш тӹнгалӹт.
Second Example Sentence: Шукы жап шып лийшӹ студент докладым каласен дӓ вуйым лӱктӓш тӹнгалын.
Third Example Sentence: Калык шотеш пӓшӓ лиймекӹ, ял вуйым лӱктӓш верч иквӓреш шогалын.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive verb phrase; accusative noun 'mouth' + infinitive verb, also appears with converbal variants.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To gape in amazement, stare open-mouthed, or speak too loudly and carelessly.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: be very surprised; speak loudly or thoughtlessly
Usage Contexts: surprise, gossip, scolding, informal narration, humorous criticism
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A face-and-mouth metaphor tied to norms of modest speech; uncontrolled open-mouth behavior can signal surprise or lack of restraint.
First Example Sentence: У уверым колмекӹ, залышты цилӓ ышмам кӓрен шӹнзӹт.
Second Example Sentence: Циц пелештышӹ эдемлан гӹнь ышмам кӓрӓш веле келеш, аргымак ак лий.
Third Example Sentence: Документ гыч санадам ужын, бухгалтер ышмам кӓрен анжале.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed existential clause; noun + focus particle + negative existential.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is so happy or inspired that only wings are missing.
Usage Contexts: good news, family celebrations, school success, theater, praise
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Bird-flight imagery connects joy with bodily lightness; it suits oral praise and narrative scenes of sudden happiness.
First Example Sentence: Ӹдӹрӹн экзамен сай лийын, тудын шылдыржы веле уке.
Second Example Sentence: Ушкалвлӓн грант шуктош, фермерын шылдыр веле уке лин.
Third Example Sentence: Театр гыч лӓктмекӹ йочавлӓн шылдыр веле уке ыльы.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Light-verb construction; accusative noun 'word' + verb 'give', with promise/speech-act semantics.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To promise firmly or formally give someone the floor to speak.
Usage Contexts: meetings, public speech, family commitments, business, formal assurances
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Speech-act idiom reflecting the social value of keeping one's word in councils, household agreements, and public meetings.
First Example Sentence: Председатель шыжӹм мостым тӧрлаташ шамакым пуыш.
Second Example Sentence: Мӹнь тылат книгӓм июнь марте пӧртылташ шамакым пуам.
Third Example Sentence: Шамакым пуаш веле агыл, тудым шотеш шындаш келеш.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative infinitive clause; noun + postposition дон + noun + comparative particle гань + infinitive.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To live in constant quarrels and hostility, like a dog and cat.
Usage Contexts: family, neighbors, workplace tension, humor, disapproval
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Household-animal simile from village life; dogs and cats are used as a vivid model of persistent conflict.
First Example Sentence: Кок сосед пи дон коти гань ӹлӓт, но пӹрыж вӓрӹште пырля пӓшӓм ӹштӓт.
Second Example Sentence: Ешыштӹ пи дон коти гань ӹлӓш йочавлӓн йӧсӧ лиеш.
Third Example Sentence: Тӓ ик кабинетеш пи дон коти гань ит ӹлӓ, мутым келшӹдӓ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative existential quantifier phrase; numeral + particle-bearing noun + negative existential.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: There is not even the smallest amount of something; nothing is left.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: there is none at all; not a single bit remains
Usage Contexts: food, money, harvest, supplies, joking complaints, household talk
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agrarian grain imagery: a single seed or grain represents the smallest useful amount in a farming household.
First Example Sentence: Амбареш вырлык ик пӹрцӓт уке, седӹндоно ӱдӹмашым вараш кодышт.
Second Example Sentence: Кошташ ак лий: карманеш тӓнгӓ ик пӹрцӓт уке.
Third Example Sentence: Мутланымаш пӹтмӹкӹ, тудын йодышлан ответ ик пӹрцӓт уке ыльы.
